verse.arabic_text: وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُsْنَىٰ فَٱدْعُوهُ بِهَا ۖ وَذَرُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِىٓ أَسْمَـٰٓئِهِۦ ۚ سَيُجْزَوْنَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ
verse.translation: And to Allah belong the best names, so invoke Him by them. And leave [the company of] those who practice deviation concerning His names. They will be recompensed for what they have been doing.

theme_question.correct: The importance of calling upon Allah using His most beautiful names.
options: The importance of calling upon Allah using His most beautiful names.
The obligation of performing pilgrimage (Hajj) once in a lifetime.
The prohibition of consuming interest (riba) in financial transactions.
The specific rewards and status reserved for martyrs in battle.

educational_notes.verse_context: This verse (7:180) appears in the latter part of Surah Al-A'raf, following detailed narratives of past prophets and their nations. It serves as a direct instruction to believers on the proper etiquette and substance of supplication. The verse emphasizes Tawhid (monotheism) by directing all worship and invocation towards Allah alone, using the beautiful names He has revealed for Himself. The warning against those who "practice deviation" (yulhidun) concerning His names refers to those who distort their meanings, deny them, or assign them to idols.
educational_notes.theme_explanation: The core theme is the concept of *Asma'ul Husna* (The Most Beautiful Names of Allah). The verse establishes that Allah possesses names of perfect beauty and meaning, and it commands believers to use these names in their du'a (supplication). This act of calling upon Allah by His names, such as Ar-Rahman (The Most Merciful) or Al-Ghafur (The Forgiving), fosters a deeper, more personal connection and understanding of His attributes. It is a fundamental pillar of Islamic theology and a key to meaningful worship.
educational_notes.surah_overview: Surah Al-A'raf ('The Heights') is the 7th chapter of the Quran, a Meccan surah that is one of the longest in the Book. It extensively details the stories of numerous prophets, including Adam, Nuh (Noah), Hud, Salih, Lut, Shu'ayb, and Musa (Moses), highlighting the consistent message they brought and the varied responses of their people. A central theme is the consequence of belief versus disbelief, illustrated through the history of past nations. The title 'The Heights' refers to a barrier between Paradise and Hell where people who are evenly balanced in their good and bad deeds will await Allah's mercy.
